metadata.file_name: 127. Sun et al. (2023). A Theoretical Framework for a Mathematical Cognitive Model for Adaptive Learning Systems.pdf
metadata.title: A Theoretical Framework for a Mathematical Cognitive Model for Adaptive Learning Systems

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: This paper is a theoretical/methodology study that constructs a mathematical cognitive model using interpretive structural modeling (ISM) based on expert questionnaires, expert interviews, and a small verbal-report validation with a single fourth-grade child. It does not analyze a nationally representative ILSA student micro-dataset and does not report any complex survey design features (e.g., stratification, clustering) or the use of student/sampling weights. Because no ILSA cycle microdata with PV-based achievement outcomes are analyzed, there is no weight variable (e.g., W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT) to apply. The data preparation consists of expert calibration (52 experts completing a cognitive-attribute relationship questionnaire) and subsequent qualitative verification via verbal reports and discussion, rather than weighted statistical estimation.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: Plausible values (PVs) are not applicable because the study does not estimate or predict ILSA cognitive achievement scores (e.g., TIMSS/PISA PV-based outcomes). Instead, it develops a cognitive diagnostic assessment framework by mapping and structuring cognitive attributes (TIMSS cognitive framework) using ISM and expert calibration, with validation via verbal reports. As a result, there is no PV generation/combination step and no PV-related missing-data strategy to report.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: The study uses a two-stage expert elicitation and validation design: (1) 33 primary school mathematics teachers, 15 graduate students, and 4 mathematics education specialists in Shanghai judged pairwise relationships among 16 TIMSS-based cognitive attributes; (2) six mathematics education professors revised the preliminary model; and (3) a fourth-grade child was randomly selected to provide an oral report to verify the cognitive model’s effectiveness. The paper does not report an analytic student sample from an ILSA dataset; instead, it reports expert counts and a single child validation case.

main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using TIMSS the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This paper proposes a theoretical framework for constructing a mathematical cognitive model intended to support cognitive diagnostic assessment and adaptive learning systems. Using interpretive structural modeling (ISM), the authors start from 16 TIMSS cognitive attributes, derive a reachability matrix from expert judgments (52 experts), and hierarchically extract a five-level cognitive model. The final model integrates TIMSS’s three cognition domains (knowing, applying, reasoning) and revises attribute relationships to improve operability and validity. Validation is conducted through expert interviews/verbal reports and a small verbal-report check with a randomly selected fourth-grade child, rather than through predictive ML performance on student achievement outcomes. No ML algorithms, PV-based achievement targets, or quantitative predictive metrics are reported. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for constructing a mathematical cognitive model intended to support cognitive diagnostic assessment and adaptive learning systems. Using interpretive structural modeling (ISM), the authors start from 16 TIMSS cognitive attributes, derive a reachability matrix from expert judgments (52 experts), and hierarchically extract a five-level cognitive model. The final model integrates TIMSS’s three cognition domains (knowing, applying, reasoning) and revises attribute relationships to improve operability and validity. Validation is conducted through expert interviews/verbal reports and a small verbal-report check with a randomly selected fourth-grade child, rather than through predictive ML performance on student achievement outcomes. No ML algorithms, PV-based achievement targets, or quantitative predictive metrics are reported.
data.null_fields_interpretation: This is a non-empirical methodology/theoretical framework paper rather than an empirical large-scale assessment analysis. It does not train or evaluate machine learning models for prediction/classification, does not use PV-based achievement outcomes, and does not report an ILSA-style analytic student sample with per-country counts or predictive performance metrics. Therefore, fields such as total_students, countries/n_students, plausible-value handling, ML techniques, and main_findings are not applicable in the sense required by the extraction schema.
